Please describe your research career to date
I aim to be an academic neurosurgeon interested in applying technology and innovation to address neurosurgical
problems. In pursuit of this, I have completed formal clinical academic training programmes throughout my
postgraduate career. I am now a run-through neurosurgical trainee (London Deanery) and an NIHR Academic
Clinical Fellow at University College London (UCL), after ranking first in Neurosurgery National Selection and
attaining the Barrow Foundation Award from the Society of British Neurosurgeons (SBNS). During this time, I have
published 70 peer-reviewed publications (including within Nature Medicine), and attained multiple fellowships and
grants. Completing a PhD is the next step in my development and training as a surgeon-scientist.
Through needs analysis, targeted innovation, and systematic evaluation I have gained a skill set in surgical
innovation. This began as a visiting medical student during a research elective at the Hamlyn Centre, Imperial
Colle

secarb__001_b.text:
ge London in 2016. I conducted a literature review of unmet clinical needs, followed by a technological gap
analysis, using this to collaborate with engineers and conceive a robotic platform which used a parallel tendon
manipulator within a tubular retractor system. We then generated a prototype, and executed a comparative
preclinical validation study, presenting the findings internationally via a clinical and engineering conference. This
project was my first exposure to the principles of biological design and interdisciplinary collaboration, and it
allowed me to discover a creative outlet which I have exercised since. My supervisor, Mr Hani Marcus, has
remained my mentor and role model since (8 years later).
I developed these skills further during my Academic Foundation Programme (Clinical Neurosciences) at the
University of Cambridge. I applied novel robotic transcranial Doppler technology with finger plethysmography to
noninvasively assess cerebral autoregulation in patients with post-concussive syndrome. I established an unmet
need through a literature review, and translated this technology via a first-in-human pilot study - after attaining
ethical approvals and collaborating w

secarb__001_c.text:
ith brain physicists.
I then joined WEISS (Wellcome/EPSRC Centre for Interventional and Surgical Sciences) UCL as a clinical
research fellow in AI and neurosurgery, where I developed an AI model capable of workflow recognition for
endoscopic pituitary surgery in collaboration with engineers and industry. This was the first of its kind in pituitary
surgery, and I then attained an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship and Cancer Research UK Predoctoral
Fellowship (£25000) to develop these AI workflow models further, publishing numerous peer-reviewed articles, for
example. I have also delivered multiple podium presentations on this work, including internationally at the World
Federation of Neurosurgeons Congress, supported by a nationally competitive SBNS Trainee Travelling Bursary.
This year, I will be a visiting scholar at Stanford University for 2 months after attaining a Berkeley Travelling
Fellowship (£8000, UCL & University of Cambridge), where I will externally validate our AI models and further
refine their workflow analysis performance.
Further to my skills in surgical technology, I have complementary skills in collaborative clinical research. I was a
junior principal investigato

secarb__001_d.text:
r for a multicentre observational pituitary surgery outcomes study, recruiting almost
every neurosurgical centre in the UK & Ireland. I executed this collaborative study while I held a national
leadership position (Vice Chair of the Neurology and Neurosurgery Interest Group), and leveraged this to motivate,
coordinate and collaborate with a network of >100 consultants, trainees and students. This study recruited >800
patients, representing the largest study of its kind, leading to multiple publications and presentations (for example).
For this, I was awarded the prestigious SBNS Hugh Cairns Prize, and have received a research grant from Stryker
(£50000, Co-investigator) for the next stage of this study (national patient-reported outcomes). This work has also
created an effective pituitary research network, which I continue to manage and remains productive. I have applied
these skills in teamworking across numerous other international projects, including with The Pituitary Society,
therefore establishing myself within relevant national and international pituitary research networks, which I hope to
leverage for future multicentre translation of my surgical technology work.
Additional

secarb__001_e.text:
ly, I recognise the importance of community engagement as an academic and as a clinician. In terms of
public engagement, I have showcased our AI work on the BBC, Reuters, NowThis and RadioFrance. I have also
created numerous explanatory videos of my work via the UCL Surge and PitCOP projects. For the latter, I have
received funding via the Small Acorns Fund (National Brain Appeal, £2700, Co-investigator) Public Engagement
Grant (WEISS, £2700, Co-investigator) to investigate patient priorities in pituitary adenoma research and develop
a core outcome set via consensus, as part of a research translation toolkit for my future work. In terms of research
community engagement, I co-created weekly academic “tweetorials” of contemporary pituitary research via the
British Neuroendoscopy Society Twitter for two years. I have served as a faculty member of the UCL AI in
Neurosurgery Course, where I taught about critical appraisal of clinical AI research to an international audience,
receiving excellent feedback. I also am passionate about open science, and have curated 25 open-access
annotated pituitary surgery videos as part of the International Endoscopic Vision Challenge.
Finally, I received

I have developed these skills in surgical innovation, collaborative research and community engagement whilst

secpesor__001_a.text:
Pituitary adenomas are amongst the most common brain tumours, and can cause vision problems and hormonal
imbalances. They can lead to severe health consequences and even death if not treated promptly. Surgery
through the nasal sinuses (transsphenoidal surgery) is usually the best treatment, but it's complex and requires
years of surgical training. Different surgeons have different approaches, making it hard to track their performance
and predict patient outcomes. This limits post-operative care options, like identifying low-risk patients for faster
discharge.
To begin to address these challenges we have to be able to understand and analyse the difference between
surgeries, and we can do this using a process called workflow analysis. Workflow analysis breaks down each
surgery into its basic building blocks (i.e. the surgical steps), and can be done by reviewing and annotating
surgical videos. These labels can help us scrutinise and compare each step of the surgery, and for example, help
us identify good performance. However, performing workflow analysis on every surgery performed takes a huge
amount of time and resources, and would be very difficult to do consistently over time. Art
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
ificial intelligence (AI)
can review videos and perform this analysis automatically, accurately, and quickly. This has only been possible
with recent advances in AI, which continues to rapidly develop.
Using predoctoral fellowship funding, we have built a secure and ethically approved system for collecting surgical
videos and data at our centre, the National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery. We have used this data to
build AI systems capable of automatic workflow analysis in pituitary surgery. In this proposal, I plan to develop the
next stage of this technology by developing three AI-powered clinical tools:
1) Smart Operative Notes: using AI workflow analysis to help surgeons create accurate, complete, and
standardized reports – easing administrative burdens and improving tracking of performance.
2) Real-Time Surgical Coach: providing surgeons with AI guidance during and after surgery – highlighting
upcoming steps, necessary tools, and even performance metrics.
3) Personalized outcome prediction: using AI to predict outcomes after workflow analysis of each surgery – to
enable post-operative care plans tailored to each individual patient.
I plan to develop these tools over 3
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
years. Each tool will undergo a pilot study to assess whether it's feasible and
user-friendly. This will be followed by a larger study to explore whether the tool is safe, and whether it's effective
(e.g. improves surgical outcomes). All of this will be performed at the UK’s largest pituitary centre (National
Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London), collaborating with engineers based at a prestigious surgical
innovation institute (WEISS, University College London), and some of the world’s largest medical AI companies
(Medtronic and NVIDIA).
This technology has the potential to revolutionise pituitary surgery by enhancing training, maintaining standards
and facilitating personalised patient care - aligning with the NIHR Brain Tumours Highlight Notice. Similarly, If
clinical translation is successful, these applications could be explored for other surgeries. The potential for this
work is therefore vast, dovetailing with the NIHR’s Health Data Science strategic theme and the NHS’s National
Strategy for AI.
I believe this proposal has the ingredients necessary to actualise these potential benefits – a diverse team, based
at centres of excellence, researching the fastest-grow

secpesor__001_d.text: ing technology in history, to improve patient outcomes.

secsa__001_a.text:
This proposal leverages artificial intelligence (AI) to address key unmet surgical needs, with the ultimate goal of
improving patient outcomes, using pituitary surgery as an exemplar.
Pituitary tumours are amongst the most common brain tumours, presenting in a variety of ways, including visual
decline and hormonal imbalance. Left untreated, they may result in significant morbidity, mortality and quality of life
impairment. Transsphenoidal surgery is the treatment of choice for most patients with symptomatic adenomas.
However, this is a challenging operation to perform, requiring years of dedicated training. Different surgeons
perform these surgeries in distinct ways, with variance in the recording of surgical performance and resultant
outcomes. Therefore, accurate auditing of practice is challenging. The variation in surgical performance, along with
patient differences, also makes the prediction of post-operative outcomes difficult. Consequently, it is challenging
to tailor post-operative care to each patient, for example, recognising patients who may be at low risk for
complications and safely discharged earlier.
The first step to tackling these problems is to understand how these

secsa__001_b.text:
surgeries are performed and compare the
relationships between their performance and outcomes. Operative workflow analysis enables this by breaking
down operations into the fundamental standardised building blocks, including the surgical steps performed.
Practically, this analysis is performed by segmenting surgical videos. This is labour-intensive if undertaken
manually but artificial intelligence (AI) has the potential to perform this analysis automatically, accurately, and at
scale.
We have developed pre-clinical AI models capable of recognising surgical workflow in endoscopic pituitary
surgery. The next step is to apply this technology to the unmet clinical needs of pituitary surgery, and I plan to do
this over a 3-year doctoral fellowship, guided by the IDEAL and DECIDE-AI guidelines, with 3 aims:
1) To investigate the use of AI workflow analysis to improve audit and quality improvement by assisting
surgeons in creating semi-automated operative notes. We hypothesize these will be more accurate,
comprehensive and standardised, whilst helping relieve administrative burden.
2) To examine the uses of AI workflow analysis to address the training needs of surgical teams, by presenti

secsa__001_c.text:
ng a
real-time analysis of current and upcoming surgical steps and instruments, with surgical performance metrics. We
hypothesize this will improve the educational yield from each case by orientating the team during the procedure
and by facilitating coaching by mentors.
3) To explore the use of AI workflow analysis for prognostication by linking this analysis with clinical outcomes,
allowing data-driven post-operative outcome prediction. We hypothesize this will allow more patient-specific post-
operative management.
I plan to translate each of these technologies via pilot studies (examining feasibility, acceptability and usability) and
single centre development studies (examining effectiveness and safety).
We have an established team of neurosurgeons (at the UK’s largest pituitary centre), engineers (at a world-leading
surgical innovation institute) and key industry collaborators (Medtronic and NVIDIA). Having built a secure data
pipeline, generated a large dataset, and created foundational preclinical models through an NIHR Academic
Clinical Fellowship and a Cancer Research UK Predoctoral Fellowship, I am confident I can deliver this proposal.

secdrp__001_a.text:
The overarching goal of this proposal is to clinically translate AI video analysis technology into practice, with the
ultimate aim of improving outcomes for patients undergoing endoscopic pituitary surgery resection. This builds on
work supported by my NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship and CRUK Predoctoral Fellowship which have
generated the largest known pituitary surgery video and outcomes dataset. The work will be carried out in the UK’s
highest volume pituitary surgery centre, supported by key industry partners and a world-leading surgical innovation
institute which holds related project grants to provide the technical and engineering infrastructure required. I hope
that at least one (if not all) of the three proposed work streams will be translated further in the future, via
multicentre comparative studies.
1. What are the problems being addressed?
Pituitary adenomas are among the most common intracranial tumours, with an estimated prevalence of up to
20%1, 2. They may present incidentally, through mass effect (e.g. visual decline) or hormone imbalance (e.g.
acromegaly), therefore potentially causing significant morbidity, quality of life reduction and death if left untreated1

secdrp__001_b.text:
,
3.
The gold standard treatment for most patients with symptomatic pituitary adenoma is transsphenoidal surgical
excision, with the endoscopic transsphenoidal approach (eTSA) bolstering access to these tumours4.
However, these operations are technically demanding with long learning curves, posing a training challenge for
surgeons, and therefore requiring post-training fellowships to attain the necessary clinical and technical skills5, 6.
Furthermore, there is variation in how these operations are performed (largely based on surgeon preference),
outcomes3, 7.
which exacerbates this training challenge and may result in differing Through executing a systematic
review and a large multicentre observational study, I have found significant heterogeneity in the closure phase
alone - in how it was performed, how operative details were recorded and how it affected outcomes (i.e. whether it
prevented the leakage of cerebrospinal fluid [CSF])8-12. This heterogeneity in practice and operative documentation
poses a significant challenge to the clinical audit of performances and maintaining safety. Similarly, the resulting
wide variations in outcomes, dependent on both operative and patient fact

secdrp__001_c.text:
ors, make post-operative
difficult12-14.
prognostication notoriously Therefore, patients are often monitored for 3-5 days to monitor for common
complications (e.g. sodium imbalance), and initiatives to facilitate earlier discharge or prophylactic treatments (e.g.
fluid restriction) in selected cases have not become widespread12-14. The inability to robustly predict outcomes and
stratify patients poses a fundamental barrier to these initiatives13, 14.
To begin to address these unmet audit, training and clinical effectiveness needs, we generated an international,
consensus-driven analysis of the operative workflow (surgical steps, instruments and errors) in contemporary
pituitary surgery15-17. This workflow analysis is used to systematically codify operations into their fundamental
components, to allow in-depth analysis and identification of operative variations15. This can be performed through
the segmentation of operative videos. However, done manually, this is extremely labour-intensive - posing
limitations to its upscaling and analysis. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI), more specifically machine
learning and computer vision, have allowed this operative workflow ana

secdrp__001_d.text:
lysis to be generated automatically18, 19.
Thus, we have developed novel pre-clinical AI models capable of accurately and automatically recognising
pituitary surgery workflow from operative videos – a world first for pituitary surgery20-23.
2. Importance of this research
In this proposal, pituitary surgery serves as an illustrative exemplar for exploring the benefits of this novel
technology in surgery, aligning with the NIHR Brain Tumours Highlight Notice. However, our findings could inform
improvements in the audit, training and outcomes of any type of surgery, once a data pipeline is established. The
potential for this work is therefore vast, dovetailing with the NIHR’s Health Data Science strategic theme and the
NHS’s National Strategy for AI.
3. This research in the context of existing literature
I have conducted multiple reviews exploring the above clinical problems and potential technological solutions such
as AI. This includes a large 193 study systematic review, highlighting the considerable heterogeneity in the
performance of endoscopic pituitary surgery, and resulting outcomes, using the closure phase as an exemplar8. I
explored this further via a multicentre prospective

secdrp__001_e.text:
observational study encompassing >700 cases and almost every
pituitary surgery unit in the UK and Ireland, which echoed the findings of the review and highlighted the difficulty of
predicting outcomes11, 12. When considering the reporting of baseline characteristics and outcomes more generally,
another 178 study systematic review again revealed the heterogeneity in the selection, recording and analysis of
surgery24, 25.
this data within pituitary adenoma This dovetails with wider literature on major unmet training, audit
and prognostication needs, which is rooted in this heterogeneity of practice and reporting.
Following this, I have conducted a narrative review of the potential of technological advances to address these
needs in pituitary surgery, and the general role of AI within surgical theatres of the future16, 26. Numerous
systematic reviews specifically exploring the potential of AI-driven video workflow analysis have already been
published, including Timoh et al’s 34 study review highlighting the infancy and recent expansion of this field27. This
is because the use of AI in this fashion was only recently possible, with the advent of neural networks and the
computing power

secdrp__001_f.text:
necessary to leverage them. Translating this technology for specific clinical applications (e.g.
teaching, audit and decision support) is even more novel, so there are limited previous studies on this generally,
and none of these are within pituitary surgery. Thus, this proposal seeks to generate new evidence in a rapidly
developing space. Recognising the exploratory nature of this work, I have de-risked this proposal by creating three
parallel project streams (as opposed to one).

5. Project plan
This work builds on foundations laid by my NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship (2021-2024) and Cancer Research
UK Predoctoral Fellowship (awarded March 2022, for 20,000gbp over 1 year). These programmes have allowed
me to collect pilot data (annotated videos and associated clinical data) for >200 pituitary adenoma patients, the
largest dataset of its kind. They have also supported the building of a pipeline for secure data collection (including
regional ethics committee approval and industry collaboration), and the development of novel preclinical AI models
capable of accurately and automaticall

My plan to systematically clinically translate this surgical video workflow AI technology is via three parallel work
packages (WP) over three years (Figure 1, Research Timetable Figure). This plan is guided by the IDEAL
framework for evaluating surgical innovation and DECIDE AI guidelines for early clinical studies of AI interventions,
with the ultimate goal of readying this technology for subsequent multicentre comparative studies28-31. The chosen
sample size for IDEAL Stage 1 studies (n=6) and IDEAL Stage 2a (n=30) is derived from the IDEAL Guidelines31.
The intent of these studies is rapid iterative modification (of the AI-human interface), and preparation of the
technology for future multicentre and definite comparative studies.
The WPs are listed in order of projected implementation difficulty (from easy to more challenging) and will be
staggered as such (see Research Timetable Figure). WP1 could overlap with WP2 and WP3 if needed, but WP2
and WP3 will not overlap as they may confound each other’s analysis. The chosen sample size for IDEAL Stage 1
studies (n=6) and IDEAL Stage 2a (n=30) is derived from the IDEAL Guidelines31. The intent o

definite comparative studies.
WP1: Applying AI workflow analysis to assist surgical audit.
The goal of this stage is to apply our pre-clinical AI model, which uses AI workflow analysis to recognise the steps
performed during an operation and populate an operative note template, to improve the accuracy, efficiency and
standardisation of our surgical documentation22.
The next stage of this work is a first-in-human pilot study (IDEAL Stage 1) of the feasibility, acceptability and
accuracy of AI-assisted operative notes, where the surgeon is presented with an AI-generated note immediately
post-operatively (based on the operative video). This note will be copied and pasted into our EMR after the
surgeon’s editing to ensure representativeness and add context (i.e. information not possible to glean from video
alone). Six prospective cases will used, recording the feasibility (number of AI-assisted notes completed, time
taken) and acceptability (via structured questionnaire) as primary outcomes28, 33. Additionally, accuracy,
comprehensiveness and clarity

secdrp__001_j.text:
will be assessed via an expert surgeon of the video and operative note, using
questionnaires based on our consensus framework for core surgical steps and the Royal College of Surgeon’s
guidance for operation documentation15, 34. Human factors analysis will be gathered via observation and interview,
and used to iterate the AI output interface and usability28. The success criteria for progression to the next stage are
based on these primary outcomes, if feasibility (>80% of notes completed successfully within 15 minutes) and
acceptability (>80% of participants finding the notes acceptable) are demonstrated. If the study fails on its first
trial, the intervention will be iterated and re-trialled if deemed necessary.
We will then perform a single centre prospective development study (IDEAL Stage 2a) of 30 prospective cases,
comparing accuracy, comprehensiveness and clarity to a retrospective cohort of 30 operative notes and videos
with no AI documentation support. Again, the AI-human interface will be continually improved with iterative human
factors analysis. The Data Collection section details outcome metrics in further detail. This study will provide the
necessary experience and te

chnological refinement for planning multicentre explorative (IDEAL Stage 2b) and
comparative (IDEAL Stage 3) studies. We plan to pursue these (via a dedicated project grant in the future) if the
intervention demonstrates any improvement in comparative accuracy, comprehensiveness and clarity.
WP2: Applying real-time AI workflow analysis for assisting training.
The goal of this stage is to present workflow analysis (surgical steps and instruments) to surgical teams in real-
time during an operation and post-operatively to promote reflective practice, and to assess its educational and
operational benefits. Our existing ethics approval (IRAS 127474) will facilitate this work. We have already
performed pre-clinical (IDEAL Stage 0) studies of the candidate AI model, which is now capable of accurate and
automatic surgical phase (F1 score 90%) and step (F1 score 75%) recognition (see Underpinning AI model
section)20, 21, 23.
The next stage of this work is a first-in-human pilot study (IDEAL Stage 1) of the feasibility, acceptability and safety
of real-time intra-operative workflow analysis28, 33. This would be single centre, recruiting six prospective cases31.
We will deploy our AI model v

WP3: Applying AI workflow analysis to assist surgical outcome prediction & improve clinical effectiveness.
The codification of surgical videos using workflow analysis enables outcome prediction for each surgery, which
can be presented to clinicians post-operatively as a decision-support tool16, 38-40. Supported by a previous CRUK
predoctoral fellowship and an upcoming EPSRC project grant (see Related Funding section), we have developed
and are refining pre-clinical AI models capable of outcome risk profiling through operative video workflow analysis.
The first step in clinical translation is a single centre first-in-human pilot study (IDEAL Stage 1) of six prospective
cases28, 31. After input of the operative video data, the predicted outcome profile will be displayed (via NVIDIA
Clara AGXTM) to expert surgeons, trainee surgeons and specialist nurses. As the ultimate goal of this technology in
the first instance is to support decision-making post-operatively but pre-discharge, only common inpatient
outcomes that were readily quantifiable were included (see Data Collection section)11, 36. We will explore the
perceived feasibility and acceptability of the AI display, as w

analysis28, 33. This will be used to iteratively improve the AI-surgeon display interface28, 31. The success criteria for
progression to the next stage are based on the primary outcomes - if feasibility (>80% of cases with successful
outcome prediction display) and acceptability (>80% of participants finding the intervention acceptable) are
demonstrated. If the study fails on its first trial, the intervention will be iterated and re-trialled if deemed necessary.
Next, a single centre prospective development study (IDEAL Stage 2a) will be performed to assess the potential
effectiveness and safety of the AI model display in 30 prospective cases when displayed to expert surgeons,

Underpinning AI models
Underpinning AI models for the above work packages have been developed and continue to be refined by our
WEISS20-23.
team at During this fellowship, it is likely these models will require iterative changes to improve
performance for each of the translational work packages. This will be informed by our study findings, and
supported by the continuing opportunistic collection of video and clinical data during these studies. As part of these
improvements, we will incorporate methods to enhance the explainability of the AI models, as this will aid
acceptance and trust for such clinical AI applications. The continuing support fo

secdrp__001_r.text:
r the engineering infrastructure and
personnel to implement any necessary changes to models will be via our existing group funding (see Research
Environment section below). For reference, I have included details of the development and evaluation of our AI
models below:
After informed consent, each surgical video is uploaded to Touch SurgeryTM Enterprise and annotated for surgical
steps by expert surgeons15, 20-23. The resulting annotated video database (now >200 videos) is split into training
and testing datasets (80:20 ratio, 5-fold stratified cross-validation). The videos are sampled at 1 frame per second,
serving as the visual input for a two-stage artificial neural network (ANN) which aims to predict the step shown in a
given frame. In the first stage, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is pre-trained to recognize steps and phases
from a short temporal window (1-5 frames). Due to the ambiguity of the different anatomical and instrument
landmarks visible in the dynamic field of view during different steps, a single frame or short sequence may not
carry sufficient information to aid correct classification20. To compensate for this, a second-stage Recurrent Neural
Network (RNN)

secdrp__001_s.text:
is trained to improve temporal resolution and prediction consistency. Step recognition was
primarily evaluated using F1 scores, calculated per class and then summed across classes, weighted to the
prevalence of the respective class. The pilot model achieved a 75% F1 score for step recognition with 50 videos20,
which is continually improving with increasing data input and model refinement.
This model is used in WP2 to recognise intra-operative workflow in real-time and generate performance analytics,
for example, surgical step order, duration and frequency. For the operative notes generation (WP1), an additional
post-processing stage is implemented to ensure the relationships between steps are clinically consistent, and
allow for integration with an operative note template22. For the outcome prediction model (WP2), a feed-forward
ANN takes the output of the existing CNN-RNN model as an input, in addition to post-operative data, to predict the
outcome probabilities for each patient. All ANNs will be trained using an appropriate backpropagation method (e.g.
adaptive gradient descent) to minimise the appropriate loss function (e.g. cross entropy). Hyperparameters
(including learning ra

secdrp__001_t.text:
te, and activation function) will be optimised41. Final model performance will be compared
against logistic regression, with averaged receiver operating characteristic curves created using 10-fold stratified
cross-validation. A probability cut-off point of 0.5 will classify the predicted outcome as low or high risk41.
5.1 Patient recruitment and ethical considerations
All patients and participants will be recruited from a single site National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery
(NHNN). For non-patient participants (i.e. the surgical team), all members will be invited to participate if their
workflow is affected by the AI intervention in question. Patients will be adults, with pituitary adenomas, undergoing
endoscopic transsphenoidal surgery. Only those undergoing revision surgery will be excluded (as this confers very
different appearances to the surgical video, which our models are not yet sensitive to). Regarding data diversity,
NHNN is the UK’s largest pituitary centre, with an international patient base. All consent forms and processes will
be available in non-English languages as necessary, and adaptations will be made to maximise inclusiveness if
possible (e.g. audio narr

secdrp__001_u.text:
ation for those who are visually impaired). I have also engaged in numerous public and
patient engagement initiatives (see PPI section) to maximise the accessibility of our work.
For the work leading up to this fellowship, we have attained local ethical approval (REC 21/SW/0027, UCL/UCLH
as sponsors) and local data governance and IT approvals at the NHNN. These approvals are currently focussed
on WP2, and detail the collection of operative videos and associated clinical data, development of AI models,
display of workflow analytics to the surgical team, and collection of surgical team feedback (including acceptability,
feasibility and usability). Any workflow analytics displayed intra-operatively would not be displayed to the operating
surgeon, therefore the risk of distraction is minimised. Thus, WP2 does not need any further amendments and we
are satisfied any potential ethical issues have been addressed.
However, WP1 and WP3 would require minor amendments to our existing ethics. For WP1, this would include the
display of AI-generated operative notes to assist surgeons in operative note completion. The risk here is low, as
there is a human in the loop (the surgeon) who will review

secdrp__001_v.text:
any AI output, and will be responsible for any
amendments to the note to reflect clinical events. This approximates existing practice at NHNN, where we often
use templated operative notes within our electronic medical record system that surgeons amend as appropriate,
and therefore we anticipate no/minimal additional risk with AI integration. Similarly, for WP3, we will display a post-
operative complication risk profile to clinicians, but again there is a human in the loop, and any management
decisions will ultimately be made by the responsible senior decision maker for the patient. Therefore, we anticipate
no/minimal additional risk with AI integration.
5.2 Data collection
 Feasibility and acceptability will be assessed via Acceptability of Intervention Measure and Feasibility of
Intervention Measure questionnaires (WP1/2/3)33.
 Human factors analysis will include independent observation and qualitative interview, with any safety
concerns analysed using the Systems Engineering Initiative for Patient Safety model (WP1/2/3)42.
 Educational yield questionnaires will be derived from the Intercollegiate Surgical Curriculum Programme
workplace-based assessment proforma (WP2).
 Perc

secdrp__001_w.text:
eived clinical effectiveness and safety questionnaires (WP3) will be developed specifically for this
study.
Operative performance based on video review will be assessed via a modified Objective structured

assessment of technical skill (OSAT) scoring (WP3)37.
 Inpatient outcomes (WP3) will be collected via our internal prospectively developed database, which aligns
with a previous multicentre collaborative study on patient outcomes, and includes the length of stay,
hypernatraemia (including diabetes insipidus), hyponatraemia (including syndrome of inappropriate anti-
diuretic hormone), new hormone deficit, cerebrospinal fluid leak and visual loss11, 36. In the future, we will
add longer-term outcomes (such as recurrence, biochemical remission and quality of life) as our dataset
becomes more longitudinal and multimodal (e.g. includes radiomics), but this is beyond the scope of the
current proposal.
All quantitative data will be descriptively presented, and qualitative data will be thematically analysed. Definite
comparative statistics will not be performed during these IDEAL Stage 1 and 2a studies.
Furthermore, during every study, the video and clinical data of each patient will b

secdrp__001_x.text:
e opportunistically collected to
continually improve our underpinning AI models. This will be done after informed written consent, using the
secured data collection pipeline detailed below.
5.3 Data management
During the foundational work upon which this fellowship application has been built, I have created a secure data
pipeline for the recording and storage of the data. This is instrumental in safeguarding new & existing data used to
refine AI models, and new data collected for AI intervention assessment.
Patient demographic and outcomes data are stored via an existing database in XLS format on the NHS secure
network drive at NHNN. Patient demographics and outcomes are collected by a senior clinical fellow as part of
standard care, following guidelines from a multicentre pituitary surgery outcomes study led by our group to ensure
completeness and accuracy11. Each record will be given a codified number, to allow linking to the related video,
without sharing of any identifiable information. This data will be backed up on an encrypted solid state drive (SSD)
which will be kept locked on hospital premises, in the lead supervisor’s clinical office.
Surgical videos are recorded from an

secdrp__001_y.text:
HD Storz Karl Endoscope across, and are collected using the secure Touch
SurgeryTM Enterprise platform by Medtronic. The platform fully anonymizes the videos before storing (redacting
any footage which is extranasal) and making them available on the secure cloud-based server (region-specific,
GDPR compliant, Amazon Web Server). No identifiable patient or user data will be stored on Medtronic servers.
Each video will be given a codified number, to allow linking to the related demographics and outcomes data. As
per our Medtronic research contract, we retain full and exclusive ownership rights to uploaded video data as the
research team. This data will be backed up on the same encrypted SSD which will be kept locked on hospital
premises, in the lead supervisor’s clinical office. Anonymised video annotation exports (e.g. workflow, OSATS,
etc.) will be stored on an encrypted SSD, and backed up via UCL Data Safe Haven, which offers a secure solution
for data storage, handling and analysis. For the intra-operative display of workflow analytics via the NVIDIA Clara
AGXTM,
our AI models are housed on the platform and they will receive a live endoscopic video feed, but no video
or clinical

secdrp__001_z.text:
data is stored on the platform in the process. AI intervention feedback data (i.e. feasibility, acceptability,
human factors, educational yield, perceived safety) will be codified as above and also stored on an encrypted
SSD, and backed up via UCL Data Safe Haven. Electronic data will be stored for a minimum of 10 years. Any
other identifiable data such as consent forms will be stored in a locked filing cabinet at NHNN for 10 years after
study completion.
Concerning data sharing, fully anonymized data will be made publicly available on the WEISS Open Data Server
along with the respective publications, AI models, and the policy for data sharing. The PI and supervisors will ask
potential data users to provide a brief research proposal detailing how they wish to use the data and the types of
data requested. This may form part of a data-sharing agreement. Independent oversight will be sought from the
WEISS executive committee. Study participants will be made aware of the data-sharing policies during consent.
5.4 Research environment
This proposal is enabled by the unique interdisciplinary environment at the WEISS UCL, UCL Neuroscience, and
NHNN. NHNN is the UK’s largest pituitary centr

secdrp__001_{.text:
e (~150 pituitary adenoma surgeries per year) and UCL
Neuroscience is currently rated first in Europe and second in the world by ISI Essential Science Indicators. This
neuroscience expertise dovetails with the established AI and clinical innovation expertise at WEISS. WEISS offers
a robust infrastructure for clinical translation, including state-of-the-art facilities equipped with cutting-edge
resources (e.g. high-performance computing clusters). It also has a dedicated team supporting research ethics,
clinical trials, quality management and public engagement. Engineering or technical demands of this proposal (i.e.
iterating AI models between IDEAL stages) will be supported by an existing EPSRC AIDPitSurg grant
(EP/Y01958X/1, 540000gbp, over 18 months from November 2022). My proposed PhD supervisors are co-
investigators and the senior leads on this project, which will fund two computer science postdoctoral research
fellows to drive AI model improvement. I am not listed as a co-investigator on this grant, and I am not receiving
personal support from this funding. Altogether, the integration of these centres of excellence, with key industry
collaborators, provides an exciting opport

secdrp__001_|.text:
unity for this proposal to flourish.
5.5 Project management
By the start of this proposed fellowship I would have been working at WEISS, UCL and NHNN for four years – one
as a clinical research fellow, followed by three years as an NIHR academic clinical fellow. During this time, I have
dedicated my research time to the necessary foundational work needed for this PhD proposal. Furthermore, the
last six months of my ACF will be research time spent continuing my ACF projects, which are precursors to my
proposed fellowship (see Research Timetable Figure). Throughout these four years, I have been mentored by my
proposed PhD supervisors, Mr Hani Marcus and Prof Stoyanov, who are experts in the field, and have established
relationships with key collaborators (e.g. IDEAL collaboration) and industry partners (Medtronic and NVIDIA). Their
regular supervision (see Research Support Section) and these established partnerships will be critical to delivering
the proposed research timetable.
5.6 Output and next steps
Research output from this fellowship will be published in peer-reviewed journals and presented at national and
international conferences, leveraging my track record of scientific wri

secdrp__001_}.text:
ting (see Research Background) and
research dissemination (see PPI). We will generate datasets of scientific value, which we will make accessible
where possible (see Data Management).
If a workstream demonstrates feasibility, acceptability, and potential for clinical impact, we will apply for further
funding to carry out IDEAL Stage 2b (multicentre) and Stage 3 (comparative trial) studies to explore the impact on
real-world patient outcomes across centres and generate higher levels of evidence. For this, we will seek further
project grants and formal Clinical Trials Unit support.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I recognise the importance of public and patient involvement (PPI) in research, particularly for topical research
such as AI, where I have studied wider perspectives on the technology. Applying this knowledge, I became faculty
for the UCL SURGE public engagement project, where I led the creation of an animated video on video analytics
AI research, to make it more widely accessible and relatable. More recently, I have received funding from the
National Brain Appeal (Small Acorns Fund, £2700) and WEISS UCL (Public Engagement Fund, £2700) to co-lead
international consensus studies on patient priorities and core outcomes in pituitary adenoma research. Through
this project, we have engaged with hundreds of patients through the Pituitary Foundation using accessible videos
(that I co-created), so that on completion, this work will form part of a research translation toolkit focussed on high-
priority patient needs. This work will be completed by the time of my proposed fellowship, and the research priority
list and core outcome set, encompassing patient views, will be encompass

secppi__001_b.text:
ed in the output from the proposed and
downstream projects.
For this specific proposal, I engaged with the WEISS UCL Pituitary Patient Advisory Group, which is a growing
group of pituitary adenoma patients interested in research, funded via WEISS. Two patient representatives from
this group, one of whom is also a representative of the Pituitary Foundation, have reviewed this application and I
have integrated their insights whilst refining lay summaries, study design, and project outcomes. In return for their
time and effort, they have received fair monetary numeration from WEISS. This application has also been
reviewed by the WEISS Public Engagement Manager, Simon Watt, to ensure the PPI is appropriately integrated
into this research design, technological iteration and future studies.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Throughout my fellowship, I intend to engage with the WEISS UCL Pituitary Patient Advisory Group for the six
proposed studies by asking for their insights on each study protocol. This will ensure the study design is sensitive
to

secppi__001_c.text:
patient priorities, and that the outcomes are aligned with patient interests. Even though concepts for IDEAL
stage 1 and 2a studies will be similar, I believe there is value in repeated active PPI at each stage, given how
dynamic the public outlook and views on AI are. For each protocol, at least two members of the group will be
approached, and their time will be renumerated appropriately. Similarly, during the amendment of our existing
ethics, I will consult the group to ensure any changes to the patient-facing documents are appropriate and
understandable.
Furthermore, PPI will be a cornerstone of the dissemination of work produced during this fellowship. I will apply my
skills in creating engaging informational videos to generate video summaries of each of the three workstreams,
working with the WEISS UCL Pituitary Patient Advisory Group to do so. I will share these research summaries with
the Pituitary Foundation so that they are available for interested patient members. Furthermore, I plan to engage
with the annual WEISS Science of Surgery event, which aims to disseminate the centre's research, to local and
underserved communities in London. I hope to use this as a way to disp

secppi__001_d.text:
lay our AI on a simulated model, with a
focus on exploring views and debunking myths about AI in surgery. Similarly, I will build on WEISS’s existing
collaboration with an interactive theatre company and deliver a scenario-based education tool for surgical video
collection and AI, aimed at patients and the public. Finally, I have recently shared our research via media (BBC,
Reuters, NowThis and RadioFrance) and could use this experience if the opportunity were to arise in the future.
I am confident that the integration of PPI will not only strengthen this proposal’s design, execution and
dissemination, but will also help me develop this important skill set for my future work as a surgeon and as a
scientist.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicable, see above.

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
I aim to be a high-quality academic neurosurgeon, with an emphasis on advancing surgical technology and
innovation. The natural next step in my development as a clinician-scientist is completing my PhD, to further
develop my general academic skills and technical skills (i.e. data science), whilst maintaining my clinical skills.
This proposal epitomizes my interests and ambitions, and having the opportunity to pursue it will give me the time,
practical experience and formal training to develop these further.
1) Technology Translational Skills:
The majority of my research thus far has focussed on pre-clinical technology development of new technologies
(robotics & AI) or clinical studies of established treatments and technologies. During this PhD, a key learning
outcome for me is learning how to bridge this gap systematically and safely through early clinical translation. I
hope to acquire the necessary technology translation skills in practice and via the learning opportunities available
at UCL. For example, the ACCELERATE Potential and ACCELERATE Expertise courses (via the Academic
Careers Office) to improve my translational research skill
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
s. Working closely with key industry collaborators is a
core tenet of technological translation, and I will also build these skills by collaborating with two of the world’s
largest medical AI companies (Medtronic and NVIDIA). I plan to spend 4-6 weeks on a visiting placement at Digital
Surgery at Medtronic (London office), to gain a deeper insight into the processes and perspectives of an industry
partner working with clinical academics, to allow me to collaborate more effectively with such partners in the future.
Specifically, this would involve joining the research management and product development teams with whom we
currently work. Similarly, there are also several entrepreneurship fellowships available in London (e.g. BiteLabs
HealthTech fellowship, Newton Fellowship), one of which I will apply for to broaden my translational skill set
further.
Furthermore, I plan on visiting an external centre in the USA for a short period (e.g. 4-6 weeks), to learn specific
skills in multicentre collaborative AI. I have identified potential hosts at George Washington University, whom we
collaborate with, and share interests in neurosurgical AI. Namely, Kevin Cleary, Professor of Bioengineeri
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
ng and
Daniel Donoho, neurosurgeon and founder of the Surgical Data Science Collective and the Digital Neurosurgery
Conference. They have worked together to perform multicentre AI work in the USA, and as multicentre work is the
natural next step of my proposal’s AI pipeline after this 3-year fellowship, I believe learning from their expertise
would be invaluable for my ongoing development.
2) Data Science & AI skills
Similarly, this proposal will allow me to further my technical skills in data science and clinical AI. Again, I plan to do
this in practice, and via courses offered by my institution. For example, the Clinician Coders Workshop (via the
Academic Careers Office), to improve my technical data science skills. I also plan to apply to the annual Surgical
Data Science Summer School in Strasbourg, with the support of my supervisors, both of whom have been faculty
on this renowned data science programme. Furthermore, AI is a rapidly evolving space with near-constant
advances in model development. Whilst I have basic coding skills, acquired through experiences such as R
statistical software courses via the University of Cambridge, I cannot independently build AI models, and ther
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
efore
I collaborate closely with AI engineers within our research group at WEISS. I think developing and maintaining this
ability in light of the rapidly advancing nature of the field is difficult to achieve whilst balancing clinical, surgical and
academic training. Therefore, my approach thus far has been to ensure I have enough practical domain
knowledge to communicate effectively within our interdisciplinary team and critically appraise our work. I have
done this through online learning platforms (FAST.AI) with the support of my AI engineering colleagues, and I
hope to continue to develop these skills through intermittent online and in-person courses during this fellowship.
3) General Academic Skills
Furthermore, through the UCL Doctoral Skills Training Programme, I plan to bolster my wider academic skills in
research management, data governance and scientific engagement. I plan to enrol in the UCL Healthcare
Engineering Impact Fellowship if time allows, to bolster my skills in leadership, scientific communication, and
patient and public engagement. Additionally, I am a faculty member of the UCL AI in Neurosurgery course, and will
continue to deliver this course to maintain and
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
develop my teaching skills.
4) Clinical Skills
By the start of this proposed fellowship, I would have finished my ST3 year in neurosurgery. During my PhD, I
intend on spending 2 clinical sessions per week (20%) to maintain my surgical and wider clinical skills. This would
be in the form of on-call shifts and theatre lists at the National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery. For on-
calls, I would take these on an ad-hoc basis, to allow flexibility around my academic work. For theatre lists, I will
aim to join elective pituitary surgery lists weekly – for my training and to facilitate the implementation of the
proposed studies.
Finally, after my doctoral studies, I plan to apply for an academic clinical lectureship and then academic
consultancy upon completion of my training. I intend to subspecialise in pituitary surgery - to align my clinical and
academic practice, and allow efficient and bidirectional bench-bedside innovation. This synergy will allow me to
keep a patient outcomes centred approach to innovation, and drive further translation of the proposed AI
technology to meet this goal in the future. This fellowship therefore optimally integrates my surgical and research
in
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
terests, and will provide the ideal training for me to grow as a cutting-edge surgeon-scientist.

(UCL)
Description of support provided
I have established academic and clinical mentors, whose mentorship I have benefitted from over the last few
years. I hope to continue to benefit from this mentorship during this proposed doctoral fellowship.
Academically, I have been supervised by Mr Hani Marcus since 2016, when I was a visiting third-year medical
student from Dublin and he supervised me during a research elective at the Hamlyn Centre, Imperial College
London. We executed and published a preclinical validation study of a robotic platform for use in endoscopic
neurosurgery. This was an incredibly formative placement for me - igniting my passion for surgical technology and
neurosurgery. Following this, Hani has mentored me over the last 8 years, and has been my formal academic
supervisor for 4 years at UCL, as a Consultant neurosurgeon at NHNN, an Honorary Associate Professor at UCL,
Deputy Director at WEISS, and Lead for Neu
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
rosurgery and Brain Cancer for the North Thames NIHR CRN. We
have published 41 peer-reviewed papers together, which is a testament to our working relationship, and his
supervision skills. His supervision track record includes guiding two students to PhD/MD completion on time, and
he is currently supervising 4 PhD/MD candidates. We have achieved this through monthly academic supervision
meetings, monthly small-group AI research meetings, and weekly large-group research meetings. I plan to
continue this tried and tested schedule during my proposed PhD.

Name Danail Stoyanov
Institution University College London
Position Professor of Robot Vision
Description of support provided
Similarly, I have been supervised by Prof Danail Stoyanov for the last 4 years. He is Professor of Robot Vision,
Director of WEISS, Royal Academy of Engineering Chair in Emerging Technologies and Chief Scientist at Touch
Surgery (Medtronic). He has been instrumental in my development as a surgical innovator, having published 15
peer-reviewed papers under his tutelage. He is an experienced supervisor, having been involved in the
supervision of >45 PhD students, with 19 successfully
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
defended and excelling in relevant fields within academia
and industry. He has also been instrumental in forging industry collaborations for this project. If successful, my
fellowship would be supported through monthly academic supervision meetings (joint with Mr Hani Marcus, with
whom he already co-supervises many PhD students), and by providing the necessary interdisciplinary expertise to
execute its goals.

Description of support provided
Furthermore, I have been supervised by Mr Laurence Watkins since 2021, when I entered run-through
neurosurgical training. He is the London Neurosurgery Training Programme Director and a Senior Consultant
Neurosurgeon at NHNN. He was my clinical supervisor for my first neurosurgical rotation during training, and has
guided me since during my surgical training. We have had scheduled clinical supervision and training meetings at
least bi-annually, and he has always been available ad-hoc for training and pastoral support during this time.
Resultant
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
ly, I have been able to excel in my clinical training thus far, as demonstrated by my multi-consultant
feedback reports, multidisciplinary team feedback assessments, surgical logbook numbers and ARCP reports.
During this proposed fellowship, I would plan to meet with him at least yearly (but aiming for every 6 months), to
ensure support for the maintenance of my clinical skills and support my eventual return to clinical training after my
PhD. I plan to use the Intercollegiate Surgical Curriculum Programme Objective-Setting Supervision Meeting
template (as used during my previous clinical rotations), to ensure supervision is structured and targeted to all
domains of my clinical development.
Collaborations
My proposed fellowship involves two key industry collaborators, with neither company expecting to access the
outputs of the research prior to publication. These collaborations are a huge asset to the proposed pipeline
(keeping costs relatively low, as they provide hardware and software at no monetary cost), and the future
scalability (i.e. to multicentre trials) and eventual translation of this work.
The project is primarily supported by a global leader in medical technology, servi
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
ces and solutions, Medtronic.
SurgeryTM
Medtronic (via Touch Enterprise) provide a world-leading secure data management and annotation
platform, allowing us to continually develop our state-of-the-art AI models. Touch SurgeryTM Enterprise is already
in use within the operating theatres at NHNN, with the necessary governance and biomedical approvals in place.
They have provided a letter of support as a supplier for this application.
Similarly, we will also work closely with the world leader in AI Computing, NVIDIA, who have supplied us with a
AGXTM
Clara platform and their technical support as part of a related EPSRC grant (see Research Plan). This
technology allows state-of-the-art efficient embedded computing systems with display monitors, allowing us to
store AI models and present workflow analysis outputs with clinicians. The necessary governance and biomedical
approvals are in process for the NVIDIA Clara AGXTM system.
Finally, I have also attained a letter of support from Prof Peter McCulloch, Chair of the IDEAL Collaboration and
Professor of Surgical Science and Practice at Oxford University, as an academic collaborator whom I intend to
seek regular review of my work this fel
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

lowship. This will ensure that it adheres to the IDEAL guidelines for

Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
Dear Committee Members
RE: Danyal Khan – NIHR Doctoral Fellowship
It is with great pleasure that I write this letter of support for Danyal Khan’s application for an NIHR
Doctoral Fellowship. I write this as Professor of Neuroscience and Head of the Department of
Neuromuscular Diseases at UCL Queen Square Institute of Neurology, which Danyal joined three years
ago as an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellow.
Balancing surgical and academic training is a challenging feat, however, Danyal has demonstrated
excellence across domains as evidenced by his clinical and research track record. Namely, he was the
highest-ranking candidate across the UK for neurosurgical national selection, has led a large
collaborative pituitary surgery outcomes project, has published 70 peer-r
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
eviewed publications, and has
attracted numerous grants to generate foundational work for this application.
His work is at the cutting edge of surgical data science, using artificial intelligence (AI) to analyse
operative videos and assist with audit, training and prognostication – with pituitary surgery as an ideal
exemplar. He has dedicated himself to the field of surgical AI for the last 4 years, integrating seamlessly
with an interdisciplinary team at the Wellcome EPSRC Centre for Interventional Surgical Sciences. Not
only has he acquired specific skills in clinical AI and data science, but he has also built strong
relationships with his clinical, academic and engineering mentors.
UCL, and the Institute of Neurology in particular, is an ideal host for Danyal to continue his academic
career. UCL Neuroscience is currently rated second in the world by ISI Essential Science Indicators. In
the calendar year 2021, Institute staff published 2197 papers; 85 were published in the top 50 of all
scientific journals (ranked by ISI impact factors), including Nature, Science, Lancet, BMJ and JAMA.
RAND report shows that UCL has the highest share of highly cited publications in Neurology in E
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
ngland.
A large proportion of the Institute's funding is obtained from the Higher Education Funding Council for
England. IoN was a major part of REF2021 Uo4 which showed that UCL is number one in the UK for
research power in psychology, psychiatry and neuroscience. The Institute currently holds 689 active
research projects, totalling £315 million, for research from the principal medical charities concerned with
neurological diseases, and from government agencies such as the Medical Research Council, and we
also receive significant philanthropic support. There are 143 Principal Investigators at IoN, including: 106
professors/professorial research associates and 26 emeritus professors; 12 Fellows of the Royal
Society; 32 Fellows of the Academy of Medical Sciences; 1 Nobel Prize winner.
The Institute and its partner hospital, The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, are not
only focused on providing the best possible care for our current patients, but also towards developing
new therapeutic approaches through which we will provide coming generations of patients with novel,
effective treatments. To do this, we have renewed focus on technology development to make our
treatm
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
ents safer and more effective, dovetailing with the NIHR AI research strategies.
Our Department is the ideal place to host this project and to enable Danyal to develop. To support
him, we will provide support through a number of mechanisms unique to UCL. During his PhD Danyal
will be enrolled into UCL’s Doctoral Skills programme to structure his academic and professional
development, whilst having the opportunity to engage in complementary courses and services. For
example, our Clinical Academic office offers dedicated training in data science, coding and
translational research (e.g. ACCELERATE programme). This is accompanied by multiple educational
programmes in UCL and NHNN, such as the Future of Neurosurgery seminars, London Brain School,
and UCL-Toronto Brain cancer seminars. There are also regular opportunities to showcase research
and network with international visitors, such as the Clinical Academic Training Symposium, IoN
Annual Symposium, and Victor Horsely lectures. Furthermore, the IoN offers a dedicated mentoring
programme to support early career researchers, which will be available to support Danyal in addition
to his existing mentors and supervisors.
We look forward t
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
o continuing to foster his potential to be an impactful clinical academic. The potential
of this work for neurosurgical research is huge and I strongly support Danyal’s application.
Yours faithfully
Professor Linda Greensmith
Sobell Head of Department
Department of Neuromuscular Diseases

Description: Data annotation costs
Type of cost: HEI
Please describe fully: Data annotation costs
Justification of Cost: Crucial to any AI technology is data curation and management. To create the video AI
workflow models employed in our AI interventions, we had to generate videos annotated with steps and
instruments. The accuracy of this annotation is crucial as it provides the ground truth for these supervised learning
models. However, this is time-consuming and requires technical expertise. We have established relations with 3rd
party profess

secsb__001_f.text:
ional medical data labellers (Anolytics), who have provided consistent, high-quality, high-throughput
labelling for our dataset. During this proposal, we aim to translate these AI models and perform early clinical
evaluation. In our work packages, we will 1) continually assess the accuracy of the models being delivered in our
intervention, 2) use video assessment as a study metric (WP2 only), and 3) opportunistically collect videos which
will be annotated and used to improve the underpinning AI models further. For 1), to assess the accuracy of the
underpinning AI models, we must provide ground truth labelling for the video cases that the AI workflow model will
be analysing. This amounts to 36 videos (6 for IDEAL stage 1, 30 for IDEAL stage 2) per WP, resulting in 108 total
videos. Our mean video length is each video is 78.5 minutes (i.e. 4710 seconds) in length, resulting in 4710
frames to be annotated per video, when sampled at 1 frame/second. The total number of frames for annotation is
508,680 (4710 x 108). We have been quoted a price of 0.02 USD per frame from Anolytics, therefore the cost of
ground truth annotations here is 10,174 USD. For 2), one of the outcomes of WP2 is to

secsb__001_g.text:
compare the change of
performance (using the validated OSAT scoring system) in an AI intervention cohort (n=36; 6 for IDEAL stage 1
and 30 for IDEAL stage 2) and a retrospective control cohort (n=30). This means 66 videos must be annotated for
OSAT scores, which we have elected should be done independently by expert annotators (and their scores
averaged). We have received a quote of 50 USD per video from Anolytics for this, and have been satisfied with
their annotation expertise for this task on previous projects. This results in an additional 3300 USD (66 x 50). For
3), this will be a byproduct of the annotations of 1) and 2), at no additional cost. Therefore, in total 13474 USD will
be required for data annotation, which is approximately 11736 GBP (or 3912 GBP per year across 3 years) after a
10% buffer is applied to contemporary exchange rates. By outsourcing these annotations, we can improve the
accuracy and standardisation of our data curation. Furthermore, this process is otherwise very time-consuming
and has limited educational benefit beyond the first few cases. Therefore outsourcing it will give me a lot more time
to spend on training opportunities and executing the actual

secsb__001_h.text:
proposed studies.
